text: Recursion Pattern: Core structure. Every recursive function needs: base case (termination condition) and recursive case (reduce problem toward base case). Template: def solve(n): if n == base: return base_value; return combine(solve(smaller_n), ...). Key pitfall: missing or wrong base case causes infinite recursion. Time: depends on recursion tree shape and work per level.

Recursion Pattern: Tail recursion. Recursive call is the last operation in function. Can be optimized by compiler/interpreter to iterative (tail call optimization). Python does NOT support TCO. Java and Python: convert to iterative manually. Example: factorial with accumulator. def fact(n, acc=1): if n==0: return acc; return fact(n-1, n*acc). Advantage: O(1) stack space vs O(n).

Recursion Pattern: Memoization (top-down DP). Cache results of recursive calls. Use dict or @functools.lru_cache. If subproblem already solved: return cached result. Converts exponential recursion to polynomial by avoiding recomputation. Template: memo={}; def solve(state): if state in memo: return memo[state]; result=compute; memo[state]=result; return result. Time: O(unique_states).

Recursion Pattern: Tree recursion (Fibonacci-like). Multiple recursive calls per invocation. Fibonacci: f(n) = f(n-1) + f(n-2). Without memoization: O(2^n) time, O(n) stack. With memoization: O(n) time. Recursion tree has exponential nodes but only O(n) unique subproblems. Identifies when memoization is crucial. Count calls = count nodes in recursion tree.

Recursion Pattern: Binary recursion on array (divide and conquer). Solve two halves independently, combine. def solve(arr, lo, hi): if lo>=hi: return base; mid=(lo+hi)//2; left=solve(arr,lo,mid); right=solve(arr,mid+1,hi); return combine(left, right). Recursion tree has log n levels, n work per level → O(n log n). Examples: merge sort, find max/min, count inversions.

Recursion Pattern: Mutual recursion. Two or more functions call each other. def is_even(n): if n==0: return True; return is_odd(n-1). def is_odd(n): if n==0: return False; return is_even(n-1). Elegant for alternating state machines. Compile to iterative using state machine pattern. Use for: parsing grammar rules, alternating game states, state machine modeling.

Recursion Pattern: Recursion on trees. Natural fit: tree structure is inherently recursive (each subtree is a tree). Pattern: def f(node): if not node: return base; left = f(node.left); right = f(node.right); return combine(node.val, left, right). Preorder: process node before recursing. Postorder: process after (results from children available). Inorder: between children.

Recursion Pattern: Recursion with return value vs side effects. Two styles: 1) Return result up the call stack (clean, functional). 2) Modify shared state (global/nonlocal variable) during traversal. Style 1: easier to reason about, compose. Style 2: sometimes necessary (e.g., tracking global max during tree DFS). Use nonlocal or self.result for style 2. Prefer style 1 when possible.

Recursion Pattern: Converting recursion to iteration. Use explicit stack to simulate call stack. Push initial call. While stack not empty: pop frame, process, push child calls in reverse order (right before left for preorder). For DFS: push unvisited neighbors. Avoids stack overflow for deep recursion. Python default recursion limit: 1000. Override with sys.setrecursionlimit(). Iterative is faster in Python.

Recursion Pattern: Recursion depth and stack overflow. Python: default limit 1000 (configurable). Java: depends on JVM stack size (~1000-10000 for typical methods). For deep recursion (n=10^5+): use iterative or tail recursion optimization. Signs of stack overflow: RecursionError in Python, StackOverflowError in Java. Prevention: increase limit, convert to iterative, use trampoline pattern.

Recursion Pattern: Trampoline pattern. Avoid stack overflow without TCO. Return thunk (function) instead of recursive call. Trampoline runs returned thunks until result is plain value. def trampoline(f): result=f; while callable(result): result=result(); return result. Converts deep recursion to iteration in languages without TCO. Useful in Python/Java for truly tail-recursive algorithms.

Recursion Pattern: Flatten nested structures. Recursively flatten nested lists/trees. def flatten(lst): result=[]; for item in lst: if isinstance(item, list): result.extend(flatten(item)); else: result.append(item); return result. Time: O(n) total elements. Use for: nested JSON, directory trees, AST flattening. Iterative version uses stack, pushing lists in reverse.

Recursion Pattern: Power function via recursion. def power(base, exp): if exp==0: return 1; if exp%2==0: half=power(base, exp//2); return half*half; return base*power(base, exp-1). Time: O(log n). Space: O(log n) stack. Iterative: see binary exponentiation pattern. Key optimization: compute half once, use twice to avoid recomputation (not memoization, just caching in variable).

Recursion Pattern: Tower of Hanoi. Move n disks from source to target using auxiliary peg. Recursive insight: move top n-1 disks to aux, move largest to target, move n-1 from aux to target. def hanoi(n, src, tgt, aux): if n==1: move(src, tgt); return; hanoi(n-1, src, aux, tgt); move(src, tgt); hanoi(n-1, aux, tgt, src). Time: O(2^n) moves — optimal. Minimum moves = 2^n - 1.

Recursion Pattern: Directory/file tree traversal. Recursive OS directory traversal. def list_files(path): files=[]; for item in os.listdir(path): full_path=join(path,item); if isfile(full_path): files.append(full_path); elif isdir(full_path): files.extend(list_files(full_path)); return files. Equivalent to os.walk(). Recursion naturally matches tree structure. Depth proportional to directory depth.

Recursion Pattern: Accumulator pattern for efficiency. Instead of building result in return value, pass accumulator parameter forward. def sum_list(lst, acc=0): if not lst: return acc; return sum_list(lst[1:], acc+lst[0]). Accumulator carries partial result; avoids reconstructing result at each level. Enables tail recursion transformation. Common in functional programming (Haskell, Scheme).

Recursion Pattern: Generating combinations with recursion. At each level, choose include or exclude. def combinations(n, k, start=0, current=[]): if len(current)==k: result.append(current[:]); return; if start>=n: return; current.append(start); combinations(n, k, start+1, current); current.pop(); combinations(n, k, start+1, current). Time: O(C(n,k)*k). The two-choice structure is fundamental to generating all subsets.

Recursion Pattern: String manipulation recursion. Reverse string: reverse(s) = reverse(s[1:]) + s[0]. Palindrome: is_palindrome(s) = s[0]==s[-1] and is_palindrome(s[1:-1]). These are O(n) time but O(n²) space due to string copying in Python. For efficiency, use index parameters instead: reverse(s, lo, hi). Recursive string processing is elegant but watch for performance.

Recursion Pattern: Proof by strong induction mirrors recursive correctness. To prove recursive algorithm correct: show base case works, assume all calls to smaller subproblems return correct result (inductive hypothesis), show current call produces correct result using those results. Structural induction for trees: show it works for leaf, assume it works for subtrees, show it works for whole tree.

Recursion Pattern: When recursion vs iteration. Prefer recursion for: naturally recursive structures (trees, graphs), backtracking, divide and conquer, when recursive formulation is significantly cleaner. Prefer iteration for: simple loops, when stack overflow is a concern, performance-critical code (function call overhead), languages without TCO. Rule of thumb: if you can write it simply with a loop, do so; use recursion when it models the problem structure naturally.
